Thomas Bwell and color in 18th century portrait painting. >> But who is Thomas Bardwell? Thomas Bwell was born in Suffukk northeast of London in 1704 and began his professional life as a decorative painter and color. He then went on to establish himself as a successful portrait painter and in 1756 he published a treatise on the art of painting in oils. He says the motive of my publishing is solely the benefit of the art. Such as are born with a happy genius, though destitute of a master or guide, may from these instructions acquire a competent knowledge of coloring almost without studying. His book turned out to be tremendously popular. After his death in 1767, a second edition was printed in 1773, which was reprinted twice, followed by no less than 11 pirated editions up to 1840. ## Full Transcript

Thomas Bwell and color in 18th century portrait painting. >> But who is Thomas Bardwell? Thomas Bwell was born in Suffukk northeast of London in 1704 and began his professional life as a decorative painter and color. He then went on to establish himself as a successful portrait painter and in 1756 he published a treatise on the art of painting in oils. He says the motive of my publishing is solely the benefit of the art. Such as are born with a happy genius, though destitute of a master or guide, may from these instructions acquire a competent knowledge of coloring almost without studying. His book turned out to be tremendously popular. After his death in 1767, a second edition was printed in 1773, which was reprinted twice, followed by no less than 11 pirated editions up to 1840. In Anecdotes of Painters 1808, Edward Edwards comments, "It must be confessed that the instructions contained in that short work are the best that have hitherto been published, and many young artists at the time found it useful. It's always good to have a a a quote from from a period to to be sure that um he was indeed well regarded." So as a portrait painter, Bwell was soon forgotten, but his book was held in high regard for almost a century after it was written. Very few people have heard of him today, but I first heard of uh Thomas Bwell in Dr. David Briggs's theory of color course again >> at the National Art School. And uh I was fascinated uh fascinated by it then and and and rather curious because uh a number of things that were brought up in our discussion um were quite different from from ideas that I'd heard before about palettes and colors for portraiture. Um, but it wasn't until uh that the U National Gallery asked me to do a painting demonstration to coincide with the Vai exhibition that I thought, oh, that Bwell chat probably has some some information that might be helpful for me. Uh, because I had decided to make a copy of uh Charlano's Madame to Pompador. And you can see with the Bwwell on the right that there there even though uh Blo was working in in France uh and was not necessarily aware of Bwell when you compare paintings of the same period um Pompador the pompador painting in 1754 and um the painting of Elizabeth Dobins of 1750 there is a similarity. So I thought this will be this would be a really excellent um thing to study in order to be better equipped to make my demonstration. Of course, I really rapidly realized I'd bitten off more than I could chew and uh uh found that the one-hour demonstration was was many weeks of work in the leadup and and afterwards out of my own curiosity um having having looked at Bwell's um instructions, I was f became fascinated with how helpful it had been. Um I'd mostly say 17th century painting. had never painted a portrait with such pearly 18th century coloring and was fascinated to discover the effectiveness of using a single pigment for the light parts of the flesh combined with Bardwell's special shade tint in in the first lay as she calls it and the transformative power of the virgin tints. These were all quite new concepts when I heard them from Dr. Briggs's lecture. uh but I certainly found the these uh approaches to be really useful with my my copy. So Bartwell primarily uh drew inspiration for his method from 17th century Fench Flemish and Dutch masters particularly artists who spent time in England with the exception of Rembrandt. Of course, Rembrandt is probably the artist he mentions the most in his in his book. Uh he he says, "The work the works of Van Djk and Rembrandt are the truest guides to nature. It is out of these most excellent masters that I have established my method. It is from [clears throat] their pictures I have found the first layer colors and from them I have learned the virgin tints and finishing secrets though I have always applied them to practice from nature. So he also describes a lineage of gradual decline from Rembrandt to the to to the present time when he was writing. And um he says, "I have often thought and still believe that those painters who had acquired so fine a manner of coloring might might have communicated it to posterity in writing." He he complains about this lack of generosity of the artists of the past quite frequently in his introduction. It is plain from the works of their pupils that they knew it because in their pictures we see the same sorts of colors and coloring and from the the little variety of capital colors and the same sameness of method used by them and it's not to be doubted but that the whole was contained in a few principles neither difficult nor tedious. So we can see in his introduction he's really introducing us to the idea that it shouldn't be so difficult and he his whole project with the book is to encapsulate what he sees as a method that has on the one hand been lost but he suspects is is not um something that can't be taught. you know, it is something that was passed on in the past and and he sees it as his job to try and put it in writing for for future generations. So he also states that and I believe I could convince my any artist even at twice painting ahead from the great masters that my method will certainly answer to all their variety of coloring. So he's making that claim. So what does his book uh contain? It's it's called the practice of painting and perspective made easy. And the areas of the book that we will be concerned with are uh within the art of oil sorry the art of painting in oil with the method of coloring. Principal colors used for painting the flesh. The principal tints that are absolutely necessary for painting the flesh. First painting or dead coloring. second painting, third or last painting. So these are the areas I'll be talking about. But he then also includes um painting of backgrounds, copying, uh drapery, landscape painting, and a new short and familiar account of the art of perspective. Of course, depending on where you sourced the materials, an umber from one part of Italy is not going to be the same as as the umber from another. uh the the quality of pigments was often variable, the stability of of many of the pigments as we know the lightfastness of the pigments. Uh compatibility was was often a problem. Uh certain paints were known to interact badly with other paints. Uh the availability, you know, some of the pigments had to come from far away and the cost. So all of these um problems meant that there was a a very select group of colors which were relied upon and used to their full potential. individual characters. It sorry, it's it's actually useful, I think, um, to think of the repertoire of colors as individual characters playing their part in different areas and at different stages of a painting, often used alone, tinted with white, or in simplest of mixtures. With the exception of Prussian blue, the pigments of the 18th century had been used for centuries. He says, "I see none in the pictures of Van Djk and Rembrandt, but what are common to painters." There's a great deal of continuity until well into the 19th century. Although artists continued to use the traditional palette in the 19th century, the range of options was greatly expanded through the scientific revolution and advances in industrial chemistry and through the industrial revolution and the search for new inexpensive colorants for textile manufacturing. The increase in the range of highly saturated pigments combined with new theories of color influenced by developments in color science led to an increasing tendency to see pigments as more or less pure examples of particular hues within a theoretical framework. So that I just in that in saying that I just wanted to encapsulate what I see to be the shifts that were about to occur in the 19th century so that we can contrast that with what I'm going to subsequently show you as Bwell's method or approach to color. It's probably no accident that Bardwell's treatis remained popular right up until the middle of the 19th century, but fell into obscurity just as the invention of new pigments and the development of new theoretical frameworks gained momentum and started to influence studio practice. In fact, in some of the later editions of Bardwell's book, I noticed there were there were attempts to um uh append his ideas about color with more recent notions of color theory. Uh in the traditional palette, pigments were appreciated for how easily they were made to resemble appearances. Bwell frequently refers to the usefulness of certain pigments in rendering particular features. Burnt umber for hair, carmine for lips, brown pink for the eyebrows. Although truth to nature was highly valued, good coloring was above all guided by the pursuit of beauty and was inherently selective. pigments were applied at particular stages of a layered process and were also appreciated for how beautifully they created an effect. And just to my final um points was to show that um this was a class I taught only a few weeks ago and where I chose um this beautiful fragment from a Reynolds painting and I can testify that um the students were they were all many of them were beginners. We did have some experienced painters but um they all did their very best to apply Bardwell's method and you can see one of the working pallets there and it was quite extraordinary. It was only a two-day workshop. Um but they got tremendous results from that. And the other thing I wanted to say is that um Bwell distills a tradition which has its origins in the Renaissance reaches powerful expression in the 17th century and persists deep into the 19th century. Bwell's specific interpretation of the traditional palette epitomizes and perhaps help shape 18th century style. The deep reds, lilac grays, and and pearly whites we see here all emerge effortlessly from Bwell's palette. But on the other hand, the clarity and simplicity of his method provided a powerful and versatile way to satisfy the everinccreasing demand for faithful representations of the world. And finally, um, an example of a recent painting that I did using Bwell's method. And I can um uh it it was a I had a very short deadline. It was painted in I have to admit I painted it very quickly um in two days in fact. And uh I used I used the method he he he um he mentioned so often which is not to have a brown underpainting which I would normally use but working with the shade tint as the base for the shadows and then of course subsequent glazing and so forth which you can see in the areas around the neck and um I think that's a good example that even today Bwell Bwell's method is is compelling and um helpful for painters of portraits.
